content.introduction: Stacks implement the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) discipline and are widely used for expression evaluation, function call management, and backtracking.

sections.title: 2.3.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define stack ADT operations (push, pop, top, isEmpty), LIFO ordering, and underflow/overflow conditions. Explain why stack behavior naturally models nested structures.
examples: Validate balanced parentheses in an expression
Model browser back navigation with LIFO behavior
Explain call-stack growth during recursive function calls

sections.title: 2.3.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Describe array-based and linked-list-based stack implementations. Walk through push/pop state changes and show how parentheses matching and DFS-style backtracking use stacks.
examples: Push sequence: 3, 5, 2 then pop twice and report top
Use a stack to convert infix to postfix notation
Demonstrate underflow handling on empty pop

sections.title: 2.3.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: Stack push/pop/top are O(1), while traversal is O(n). Compare fixed-capacity array stacks against dynamic linked stacks for performance and memory behavior.
examples: Compare array stack and linked stack in cache locality
Estimate memory overhead per node in linked stacks
Choose stack when only top-end operations are required

keyTakeaways: Master the stack ADT and LIFO semantics
Implement stack with arrays or linked nodes
Use stacks for nested-structure and undo-style problems
Evaluate O(1) operation costs and boundary checks
Select implementation by capacity and memory constraints
